summary: Mitchell Hashimoto explains how the APPLE customer service framework he learned as an Apple Store employee became foundational to his entrepreneurial success. The acronym guides interactions through warm approach, polite probing, presenting solutions, listening, and fond farewells. He demonstrates the principle through a real support email exchange where instead of dismissing a canceling customer, he engaged genuinely, fixed the underlying product issue, and invited them back. This philosophy helped him build three successful projects including the one that funded HashiCorp and Vagrant.
key_points: The APPLE framework (Approach, Probe, Present, Listen, End) from Apple retail training applies directly to running products and businesses
Being respectful and caring toward users is the most important factor in building successful projects, even when there's no immediate return
The best solution isn't just one that solves the customer's problem—it's the one that solves it best, often requiring extra effort
A cancellation request is an opportunity to understand, improve, and potentially win back a customer through genuine engagement
Success came from three projects: a passive income business that funded HashiCorp, Vagrant, and IRCRelay which became profitable in 6 weeks
money_quotes: When doing anything, there is nothing more important than being respectful to your customer or user.
All that matters is being a good, caring person.
A solution isn't just something that solves the customer's problem, it is the solution that solves the customer's problem best.
The solution took extra time and effort, and maybe Brian won't even sign up again. But it doesn't matter. It was the right thing to do.
I genuinely do care about making the experience the best it can be for every customer.
A little bit of APPLE goes a long way.
key_insight: Treating every customer interaction with genuine care and solving problems at their root—not just addressing symptoms—is what separates successful products from failed ones.
